summary: O'Neil examines the arms race between Twitter and spam bots that inflate follower counts, framing it as a 'modeling war' between sophisticated parties. She notes that follower count has become a flawed proxy for influence, creating a market for fake accounts. Despite having massive resources post-IPO, Twitter appears to have reduced efforts to combat spam. O'Neil argues this is shortsighted: while Twitter may feel protected by lack of competition, the degraded user experience from spam could ultimately drive users away entirely.
key_points: Follower count as an influence metric creates perverse incentives to game the system through fake accounts
Twitter's anti-spam efforts have reportedly declined despite having substantial resources to combat the problem
The lack of competing platforms may be making Twitter complacent about maintaining quality
User experience degradation from spam is a more fundamental threat than reputational damage from a gamed system
Modeling wars exist not just between corporations and an unwitting public, but between sophisticated adversaries
money_quotes: The modelers are working hard trying to convince or trick the public into clicking or buying or consuming or taking out loans or buying insurance, and the public is on the other, barely aware that they're engaging in anything at all resembling a war.
It's a measure of a person's influence, albeit a crappy one for various reasons (and not just because it's being gamed).
The high impact of the follower count means it's in a wannabe celebrity's best interest to juice their follower numbers, which introduces the idea of fake twitter accounts to game the model.
If everyone knows how gamed the system is, they'll stop trusting it.
That argument only really holds if people have something else to use instead as a better proxy of influence.
At some point, if Twitter doesn't maintain standards, people will just not spend time on Twitter, and its proxy of influence will fall out of favor for that more fundamental reason.
key_insight: When a metric becomes a target, it ceases to be a good metric—and platforms that rely on gameable proxies for influence risk losing users not because competitors emerge, but because the degraded experience makes engagement pointless.
